title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design

yaml.title: Sampling Theory, Power Analysis & Cluster RCT Design
yaml.description: Monte Carlo simulation across 13,000 regressions to demonstrate sampling noise, superpopulation inference, individual and cluster-level power calculations with ICC and attrition adjustments.

markdown.headingText: Part 1: Sampling noise in a fixed population
markdown.markdown:
```{=html}
Stata · simulate Monte Carlo methods Power analysis Cluster RCT design
Course
PPOL 6818 · Georgetown University
Total simulations
13,000 regressions
Tools
Stata, simulate, postfile
GitHub
View code ↗
``` Generated a fixed population of **10,000 individuals** with a true Y–X relationship (seed-fixed for reproducibility), then ran 500 regression simulations at four sample sizes to quantify how estimation precision changes as we draw larger subsamples from a bounded population. **Key finding:** At N=10, beta estimates vary wildly across simulations despite being unbiased on average. At N=10,000 (the full population), all sampling variance disappears entirely — this is the finite-population ceiling. | Sample size (N) | Mean β̂ | Avg. SEM | 95% CI width | % significant | |---|---|---|---|---| | 10 | ≈ true β | High | Very wide | ~30–40% | | 100 | ≈ true β | Moderate | Wide | ~70–80% | | 1,000 | ≈ true β | Small | Narrow | ~98%+ | | **10,000** | **= true β** | **Near zero** | **Minimal** | **100%** | ## Part 2: Sampling noise in an infinite superpopulation Extended the simulation to an infinite superpopulation DGP — generating **fresh data each run** — across **26 sample sizes**: all 20 powers of 2 (4 → 524,288) plus N = 10, 100, 1,000, 10,000, 100,000, and 1,000,000. **Why superpopulation allows N = 1,000,000:** In Part 1 the population is capped at 10,000. The superpopulation DGP draws from an infinite distribution each run — sample size is unbounded. **SEM difference:** In the superpopulation, variance shrinks as 1/√N but never hits zero. At the same N, superpopulation SEM is slightly larger than fixed-population SEM, because the fixed population's known finite structure removes a layer of uncertainty. ## Part 3: Individual-level power calculations Designed a DGP where Y ~ N(0,1), treatment effect = **0.1 SD** (uniform 0.0–0.2 SD), 50/50 treatment-control split. Calculated minimum sample sizes for **80% power** under three scenarios: ```{=html}
~3,142
Baseline N (80% power, 0.1 SD effect)
~3,697
After 15% attrition (+18% larger)
~4,190
30% treatment arm (unequal split)
``` **Attrition:** Losing 15% of participants symmetrically inflates required sample by ~18% — you must recruit enough to *still have sufficient power after dropout*. **Unequal arms:** Shifting to 30/70 treatment-control requires a larger total N. The smaller arm is the binding constraint on precision. The 50/50 split is always the most efficient design for a fixed total N. ## Part 4: Cluster RCT design — ICC = 0.3 Designed a school-based intervention where treatment is assigned at the **school level** — not student level. Treatment effect = 0.2 SD (uniform 0.15–0.25 SD), ICC/ρ ≈ 0.3. **Design effect at 15 students/school:** DEFF = 1 + (15–1) × 0.3 = **5.2** — meaning you need roughly 5× as many observations as an individual-level RCT. **Power plateaus quickly:** With 200 schools fixed, power reaches ~80% around 8–16 students/school. Beyond that, adding students yields near-zero gains due to high ICC. | Scenario | Schools needed | Total students | |---|---|---| | Full compliance (100% uptake) | ~100 schools | ~1,500 | | **70% adoption (partial compliance)** | **~145 schools** | **~2,175** | **Partial compliance finding:** When only 70% of assigned schools implement the intervention, the required school count rises by **~45%**. Non-compliance is a design problem, not just a fidelity problem. ```{=html}
Skills demonstrated
Monte Carlo simulation Stata simulate command RCT design Power analysis Cluster randomization ICC / design effect Attrition modeling Reproducible code
```
